stations.label: THE NEW CURRENCY ACT
stations.line: In 1871 they gave me a decimal spine and a round name, and I began to count the country into being.
stations.era_photo: A young woman in early Meiji formal kimono standing beside a Meiji government minting building in Osaka, stiff wet-collodion tintype, sepia tones, long exposure holding her perfectly still.

stations.label: I ENTERED THE GOLD
stations.line: In 1897 I was bound to gold, and for the first time the world agreed on what I was worth.
stations.era_photo: Standing at a Bank of Japan counter in a dark high-collared Meiji Western-influenced dress, albumen print, formal frontal composition, gold ledgers visible on the table.

stations.label: FIXED AT 360
stations.line: In 1949 they pinned me at three hundred sixty to the dollar, and I learned to serve a rebuilding I did not choose the terms of.
stations.era_photo: A woman in austere postwar cotton clothing amid Occupation-era Tokyo rubble and reconstruction scaffolding, black-and-white silver gelatin print, harsh documentary daylight.

stations.label: THE PEG BREAKS
stations.line: In 1971 the dollar let go of gold, and for the first time I was permitted to move — and I moved upward.
stations.era_photo: Standing near a Nihonbashi trading floor in a muted 1970s wool suit-dress, grainy Kodachrome color, teletype machines and paper tape blurred behind her.

stations.label: THE PLAZA ACCORD
stations.line: In 1985 five men signed a paper in New York and I doubled in strength within three years; they called it agreement, and I was the price of it.
stations.era_photo: A composed woman in a sharp 1980s tailored blazer in a Tokyo office of glass and steel, saturated 35mm color film, bubble-era neon reflected in the window at dusk.

stations.label: THE BUBBLE ENDS
stations.line: In 1990 the asset prices collapsed, and what followed was not a crash but two decades of a country quietly discovering the flaws in what it had believed.
stations.era_photo: Still, unsmiling woman in a dark restrained suit before a falling Nikkei board, cool early-90s color photography, fluorescent light, muted palette.

stations.label: THE CARRY BEGINS
stations.line: In 2013 they set my rate near zero and left it there, and I became the funding the whole world borrowed without asking what the years cost me.
stations.era_photo: Woman in minimal charcoal modern attire in a quiet Bank of Japan corridor, clean digital DSLR image, soft shadowless institutional lighting, glass and pale wood.

stations.label: THE STRUCTURE BECAME VISIBLE
stations.line: On the fifth of August the carry unwound in a single day, and the patience of eleven years completed itself; I would not call it revenge, only the structure becoming visible.
stations.era_photo: A perfectly still woman in precise contemporary black, standing before a plunging market display, high-resolution digital photograph, cold blue screen-light on her composed face.

stations.label: TODAY, STILL COUNTING
stations.line: Today I hold a rate no longer zero and a discipline no longer questioned, and I remain what I have always been — present, at a price.
stations.era_photo: Woman in immaculate modern minimalist dress in a serene Tokyo interior with a shakuhachi resting nearby, crisp full-color digital photography, natural window light, deep quiet space around her.
